title: Ride Sharing Service
prompt: Design a ride sharing service like Uber or Lyft. Riders should be able to request a ride, the system matches them with nearby drivers, provides real-time ETA and tracking, and handles dynamic pricing (surge pricing) during high-demand periods.

functional: Riders request rides by specifying pickup and dropoff locations
Match riders with the nearest available driver
Real-time location tracking of driver en route and during trip
Dynamic/surge pricing based on supply and demand
Trip history and receipts for riders and drivers
nonFunctional: Matching latency < 10 seconds in normal conditions
High availability (99.99%) — downtime means stranded riders
ETA accuracy within 20% of actual arrival time
Surge pricing updates within 1 minute of demand changes

scale.qps: 5K ride requests/sec, 1M location updates/sec from active drivers
scale.storage: 100 TB trip history, 10 TB location data per day

keyQuestions: What types of rides do we support (solo, shared/pool, scheduled)?
How should we handle areas with no nearby drivers?
Do we need real-time tracking during the trip?
How granular should surge pricing be (city-wide, zone-based, hex-based)?
Do we need to support scheduled rides in advance?
What payment methods do we support?

keyCalculations: Ride request QPS: 20M trips/day / 86400 = ~230/sec avg, 5K/sec peak
Location updates: 5M drivers * 20% active = 1M drivers sending updates every 4 sec = 250K/sec
Geospatial index size: 1M active drivers * 100 bytes = 100 MB (fits in memory)
Location history: 1M drivers * 1 update/4s * 50 bytes = 12.5 MB/sec = 1 TB/day
ETA computation: need routing engine capable of 10K route calculations/sec

expectedEndpoints: POST /api/v1/rides/estimate { pickup, dropoff } => { estimatedFare, surgeMultiplier, eta }
POST /api/v1/rides/request { pickup, dropoff, rideType } => { rideId, status, driverInfo }

expectedEntities: riders: id, name, email, phone, payment_method_id, rating
drivers: id, name, vehicle_info, license, rating, status (available/busy/offline)
rides: id, rider_id, driver_id, pickup_location, dropoff_location, status, fare, surge_multiplier, created_at, started_at, completed_at
driver_locations: driver_id, lat, lng, heading, speed, timestamp (time-series)
surge_zones: zone_id (H3 hex), demand_count, supply_count, surge_multiplier, updated_at
payments: id, ride_id, amount, method, status, processed_at

expectedComponents: Location Service (ingest and index driver locations)
Geospatial Index (QuadTree, GeoHash, or H3 hexagonal grid)
Surge Pricing Service (supply/demand ratio per zone)
Notification Service (push notifications to rider and driver)
Real-time Tracking (WebSocket gateway for live updates)

suggestedTopics: Geospatial indexing: QuadTree vs GeoHash vs H3 for driver location lookup and surge zones
Matching algorithm: nearest-driver vs dispatch optimization (minimize total wait time across all requests)
Surge pricing: supply/demand ratio computation, smoothing to prevent oscillation, zone granularity
ETA calculation: pre-computed routing tables, real-time traffic adjustment, ML-based predictions
Location ingestion at scale: handling 250K updates/sec with in-memory geospatial index
Consistency: what happens when two riders are matched to the same driver simultaneously

checklist: Asked about ride types (solo, shared, scheduled)
Asked about geographic scope and driver density

anchors.1: Skipped requirements, jumped to database schema without understanding matching or pricing needs
anchors.2: Asked about basic ride flow but missed real-time or surge pricing requirements
anchors.3: Covered matching, tracking, surge pricing, and scale with reasonable estimates
anchors.4: Proactively identified edge cases: no nearby drivers, concurrent matching conflicts, ride cancellations, and payment failure handling

checklist: Included geospatial index for driver lookup
Designed surge pricing as independent service

anchors.1: Simple CRUD app with database queries for nearest driver, no real-time component
anchors.2: Basic matching with database queries, no geospatial index or real-time tracking
anchors.3: Geospatial index, separate matching and location services, WebSocket for tracking, surge pricing service
anchors.4: Optimized dispatch with batch matching, H3-based surge zones, pre-computed routing, and clear failure handling

checklist: Addressed surge pricing computation and smoothing

anchors.1: No depth on geospatial indexing, matching, or pricing algorithms
anchors.2: Basic geospatial discussion but no matching optimization or surge pricing details
anchors.3: Deep dive into geospatial index + matching or surge pricing with concrete algorithms
anchors.4: Thorough analysis of H3 indexing, dispatch optimization, surge smoothing algorithms, and ML-based ETA with failure mode handling

checklist: QuadTree vs GeoHash vs H3 for geospatial indexing
Push vs pull for driver location updates

anchors.1: Could not identify any trade-offs in the design
anchors.3: Discussed 2-3 trade-offs with pros/cons covering matching, pricing, or geospatial
anchors.4: Systematically evaluated geospatial, algorithmic, and real-time trade-offs with latency and fairness analysis

commonMistakes: Using SQL queries with distance calculations instead of a geospatial index for driver lookup
Not handling the race condition where two ride requests match to the same driver
Implementing surge pricing at city level instead of fine-grained zones (H3 hexagons)
Polling for driver location instead of using push-based updates
Not considering what happens when no drivers are available in the area
Ignoring ETA accuracy — assuming straight-line distance instead of actual road routing
